entities.label: HTTP 401 Unauthorized

claims.claim: Use 401 when caller has not authenticated or token is missing

entities.notes: src-02 explicitly closes the expired/malformed edge cases left implicit by src-01. Combined claim is normative.

entities.label: HTTP 403 Forbidden

claims.claim: Use 403 when caller is authenticated but lacks permission for the resource
Do not use 403 for expired or malformed tokens

entities.notes: 403 scope is strictly post-authentication. Using it for token failures is explicitly prohibited.

entities.label: WWW-Authenticate Header

claims.claim: Return WWW-Authenticate headers with 401 responses when the auth scheme requires credential retry

entities.notes: Conditional on auth scheme supporting credential retry. Not addressed by src-02; no conflict.

entities.label: Token Lifecycle
entities.notes: Advisory/best-practice weight. Only preventive (design-time) claim in the source set; all others are reactive (error-response).

entities.label: Authentication Failure

claims.claim: Authentication failure encompasses: missing token, invalid token, expired token, malformed token
Authentication failure must be distinguished from authorization failure in operator-facing documentation

entities.notes: This entity is the conceptual anchor for the 401 vs 403 decision boundary.

entities.label: Authorization Failure

claims.claim: Authorization failure means caller is authenticated but lacks permission
Authorization failure must be distinguished from authentication failure in operator-facing documentation

entities.notes: Counterpart to ent-authn-failure. Maps exclusively to HTTP 403.

relationships.note: All authentication failure conditions map to 401
401 conditionally carries WWW-Authenticate when auth scheme supports retry
Short-lived tokens reduce the window for expired-token authentication failures

coverage_gaps: Refresh token flows and 401 signalling when refresh is possible
Rate-limiting (429) interaction with auth retry loops
